original_abstract: The obstacles judges face in balancing the principles of legal certainty, justice, and utility can be broadly categorized into internal and external factors. Internal obstacles originate from within the judicial figure and the immediate professional framework; these include the integrity of the recruitment and appointment process, the quality of formal education and ongoing training, the depth of scientific and legal knowledge, personal morality, and the adequacy of the judge's welfare. Conversely, external obstacles arise from broader systemic and environmental pressures. These encompass the actual independence of judicial power from political or hierarchical interference, the quality and clarity of legislative formulations, the efficiency of the prevailing judicial administration, the level of community participation and public trust, and the effectiveness of oversight mechanisms. This study identifies these challenges as critical barriers to achieving a cohesive and fair civil justice system.
humanized_abstract: The obstacles that a judge has to encounter in trying to balance the principles of legal certainty, justice and utility can be broken down into two levels: internal and external obstacles. Internal obstacles are those of a psychological nature that affect the judicial figure itself and the immediate professional framework in which it is inserted: the selectivity of the recruitment and selection processes; the quality of judicial training; the extent of specialist and legal knowledge; the judge’s personal morality; the judge’s state of welfare. External obstacles on the other hand are those that affect the judiciary in a more structural or environmental way, and therefore it is possible to include the real independence of the judiciary and the absence of political and hierarchical interference; the quality and clarity of the wording of the laws and regulatory provisions; the organisation of the judiciary and efficiency of the administration of justice; the degree of participation and trust of civil society; the efficiency of mechanisms for monitoring the activity of the judiciary. According to this research, obstacles to a fair and coordinated justice system are of great importance.
